text: The laparoscopic anterior resection is performed for an upper rectal carcinoma in a patient with situs inversus. The laparoscope is positioned laterally to the left of the umbilicus, with surgical ports in the left iliac fossa and left upper quadrant. An assistance retraction port is placed lateral to the umbilicus on the right. The splenic flexure is observed in the right upper quadrant and the liver in the left upper quadrant. The sigmoid colon is lifted from the pelvis, revealing the tumor in the upper rectum marked by a tattoo. Omental adhesions between the right anterior abdominal wall and the colon are divided.

The laparoscopic anterior resection for upper rectal carcinoma in a patient with situs inversus begins with port placement mirrored from the usual setup. The laparoscope is positioned laterally to the left of the umbilicus, with surgical ports in the left iliac fossa and left upper quadrant. An assistance retraction port is placed laterally to the umbilicus on the right. Initial laparoscopy reveals the splenic flexure in the right upper quadrant and the liver in the left upper quadrant. The sigmoid colon is lifted from the pelvis, exposing the tumor in the upper rectum marked by a tattoo. Omental adhesions between the right anterior abdominal wall and the colon are divided. The omentum and transverse colon are reflected upwards, and the small bowel is displaced into the left upper quadrant to expose the operative field. Unusual adhesions between the sigmoid mesocolon and posterior peritoneum are divided, allowing elevation of the sigmoid colon to display the sacral promontory. Dissection proceeds with a harmonic scalpel, starting anterior to the promontory and moving cephalad towards the inferior mesenteric artery, which is controlled with hemlock clips and divided with the harmonic scalpel.

The surgeon reflects the omentum and transverse colon upwards, displacing the small bowel into the left upper quadrant to expose the operative field. Unusual adhesions between the sigmoid mesocolon and posterior peritoneum are divided, allowing elevation of the sigmoid colon to display the sacral promontory. Dissection is performed with a harmonic scalpel, starting anterior to the promontory and proceeding cephalad towards the origin of the inferior mesenteric artery. The artery is displayed, controlled with hemlock clips, and divided using the harmonic scalpel.

The surgeon elevates the sigmoid colon anteriorly, exposing the gonadal vessels and right ureter. Dissection continues along the taut fascia, mobilizing the descending colon over the perinephric fat until the colon is fully mobilized.

The surgeon elevates the sigmoid colon anteriorly, exposing the gonadal vessels and right ureter. Dissection continues along the taut fascia to mobilize the descending colon, progressing over the perinephric fat. The lateral division of the peritoneum in the right paracolic gutter completes the mobilization of the descending colon.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the descending colon by dividing the peritoneum in the right paracolic gutter. After mobilizing the splenic flexure, the dissection focuses on the pelvis, starting on the left side of the mesorectum and displacing it forward, then continuing on the right side medial to the right ureter. The dissection progresses in the mesorectal plane until the rectum is fully mobilized. The distal mesorectum is divided before transecting the rectum at the junction of the middle and lower third.

After mobilizing the splenic flexure, the surgeon begins dissection in the pelvis on the left side of the mesorectum, displacing it anteriorly. The dissection proceeds on the right side, medial to the right ureter, continuing in the mesorectal plane until the rectum is fully mobilized. The distal mesorectum is divided before transecting the rectum at the junction of the middle and lower third.

The surgeon exteriorizes the tumor through an umbilical incision and resects it. The proximal colon is returned to the abdominal cavity with the anvil in place. A staple gun is advanced through the rectum near the left lateral end of the staple line, and the anvil is coupled. The gun is closed and fired to perform the colorectal anastomosis.

The surgeon exteriorizes and resects the tumor through an umbilical incision. The proximal colon is returned to the abdominal cavity with the anvil attached. A staple gun is advanced through the rectum near the left lateral end of the staple line, and the anvil is coupled. The gun is closed and fired to complete the colorectal anastomosis.
